en.extract:
The Timor boobook (Ninox fusca) is a species of owl in the family Strigidae. It is found on Timor, Roma, Leti and Semau islands in the eastern Lesser Sunda Islands.
It has a more grey-brown plumage with no red tinge, unlike other subspecies. It has grey streaks on its belly and white spots on its secondaries, inner wing-coverts and nape. It was described by French ornithologist Louis Pierre Vieillot in 1817 as Strix fusca. Austrian ornithologist Carl Eduard Hellmayr noted that it closely resembled the Australian boobook and concluded it was probably a subspecies of the latter, and Mayr classified it as a subspecies in 1943. Genetic and call analysis show it to be markedly divergent to the Australian populations of the Australian boobook, leading Gwee and colleagues to suggest it be reclassified as a separate species. Its calls are shorter and more frequent than the Australian boobook. It was reclassified as a distinct species in 2019.

The '''Timor boobook''' ('''''Ninox fusca''''') is a species of [[owl]] in the family [[Strigidae]]. It is found on [[Timor]], Roma, [[Leti (island)|Leti]] and [[Semau]] islands in the eastern [[Lesser Sunda Islands]].<ref name="König 2009"/><ref name=johnstone96>{{cite journal |last1= Johnstone |first1= R. E. |last2= Darnell |first2= J. C. |year= 1997 |title= Description of a new subspecies of boobook owl ''Ninox novaeseelandiae'' (Gmelin) from Roti Island, Indonesia |journal= Western Australian Naturalist |volume= 21 |issue= 3 |pages= 161–74 |doi= 10.5962/p.310365 |url= http://www2.nrm.se/ve/birds/sape/GlobalOwlProject/Ninox/Ninox%20boobook%20rotiensis%20%28Johnstone%20%26%20Darnell%29%201997%2C%20Western%20Australian%20Naturalist%2C%2021%2C%20pages%20161-.PDF |archive-date= 2020-09-25 |access-date= 2019-01-20 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20200925172424/http://www2.nrm.se/ve/birds/sape/GlobalOwlProject/Ninox/Ninox%20boobook%20rotiensis%20%28Johnstone%20%26%20Darnell%29%201997%2C%20Western%20Australian%20Naturalist%2C%2021%2C%20pages%20161-.PDF |url-status= dead }}</ref>

It has a more grey-brown plumage with no red tinge, unlike other subspecies. It has grey streaks on its belly and white spots on its secondaries, inner wing-coverts and nape.<ref name="König 2009">{{cite book|author1=König, Claus |author2=Weick, Friedhelm |author3=Becking, Jan-Hendrik |title=Owls of the World|publisher=A&C Black|date=2009|series=Helm Identification Guides|pages=457–59|isbn=978-1-4081-0884-0|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Rnz1c8olgWcC&pg=PA459}}</ref> It was described by French ornithologist [[Louis Pierre Vieillot]] in 1817 as ''Strix fusca''.<ref>{{cite book | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/18035768 | title= Nouveau dictionnaire d'histoire naturelle, appliquée aux arts, à l'agriculture, à l'économie rurale et domestique, à la médecine, etc.  | year=1817 | volume = 7 | page=22 | location=Paris | publisher=Chez Deterville | language=French | last=Veillot | first=Louis Pierre | doi= 10.5962/bhl.title.20211 }}</ref> Austrian ornithologist [[Carl Eduard Hellmayr]] noted that it closely resembled the [[Australian boobook]] and concluded it was probably a subspecies of the latter,<ref>{{cite book|last1=Hellmayr|first1=Carl Eduard|last2=Haniel | first2=C.B. | language=German |title=Die Avifauna von Timor|date=1914|location=Stuttgart|page=102| publisher=Im Kommissionsverlag der E. Schweizerbartschen Verlags Buchhandlung|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/33606516}}</ref> and Mayr classified it as a subspecies in 1943.<ref name="mayr">{{cite journal|last=Mayr|first=Ernst|date=1943|title=Notes on Australian Birds (II)|journal=Emu|volume=43|issue=1|pages=3–17|doi=10.1071/MU943003|bibcode=1943EmuAO..43....3M|url=http://www2.nrm.se/ve/birds/sape/GlobalOwlProject/Ninox/Ninox%20boobook%20moae%20(Mayr)%201943,%20Emu,%2043,%20pages%2013-14..PDF|archive-date=2017-09-23|access-date=2019-01-20|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170923051136/http://www2.nrm.se/ve/birds/sape/GlobalOwlProject/Ninox/Ninox%20boobook%20moae%20(Mayr)%201943,%20Emu,%2043,%20pages%2013-14..PDF|url-status=dead}}</ref> Genetic and call analysis show it to be markedly divergent to the Australian populations of the Australian boobook, leading Gwee and colleagues to suggest it be reclassified as a separate species.<ref name=gwee2017>{{cite journal | title=Bioacoustic and multi-locus DNA data of ''Ninox'' owls support high incidence of extinction and recolonisation on small, low-lying islands across Wallacea  | last1=Gwee| first1= Chyi Yin | last2=Christidis| first2= Leslie | last3=Eaton| first3= James A. | last4=Norman| first4= Janette A.| last5=Trainor| first5= Colin R.| last6=Verbelen|first6= Phillippe | last7=Rheindt|first7= Frank E. | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |year= 2017 |volume=109 | pages=246–58 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2016.12.024 | pmid=28017857| bibcode=2017MolPE.109..246G}}</ref> Its calls are shorter and more frequent than the Australian boobook.<ref>{{cite journal | url=http://www.globalraptors.org/grin/researchers/uploads/491/timor_southern_boobook.pdf | title=Is the Timor southern boobook a separate species? | first1=Jerry | last1=Olsen | first2=Stephen | last2=Debus | date=2010 | volume=28 | issue=1 | page=10 | journal=Boobook | archive-date=2017-09-16 | access-date=2019-01-20 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170916053345/http://www.globalraptors.org/grin/researchers/uploads/491/timor_southern_boobook.pdf | url-status=dead }}</ref> It was reclassified as a distinct species in 2019.
